reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper provides a practical guide on how to train an NN-based controller for locomotion tasks with robustness and efficiency. 

The tasks are categorized into motion primitives including running, jumping, and crawling, each with a manually designed objective function. Considering the periodic nature of locomotion, the author proposed to use sine activation layers and periodic signals in different phases as input. The experiments show that a larger batch size can lead to faster convergence. Compared to SGD, this paper points out that Adam is better for the tasks. The supplementary video presents that the trained networks enable real-time control of complex skills.
reviews.main_review:
Based on existing differentiable physics engines, this paper further gives empirical recipes on how to use the physics gradients to train powerful controllers, which can help the community to develop techniques related to differentiable physics. 

This method divides long-horizon tasks into smaller periods with different objectives. To avoid sparse and non-smooth rewards, objective functions are manually designed for subtasks. Periodic control input is also added to the input to actuate the controller. When training the network, the authors use batching, regularization terms, and sine activation layer to accelerate and stabilize the convergence.

When reading this paper, I have several concerns,
1. In Equation 5, how is $g_v(n)$ subtracted from $A(t)$? Do they have the same dimension? $g_v(n)$ as a velocity should have a dimension of 3 (or 6 if rotation is included). The dim of  $A(t)$ should be the DOF of actuation. Maybe you mean $||g_v(n)||$. Why only target velocity is considered, how about the height $g_h(n)$? Why “periodic control signal may induce unwanted high-frequency noises”？
2. The paper mentions "a modified Adam optimizer". I did not find how Adam is modified.
3. In Figure 4, what is the definition of one iteration? Is it one step of network update or one step of simulation?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper gives us a good example of how to train a network using differentiable physics. Their video of interactively controlling 2D and 3D robots looks interesting. Although some of the techniques like staging, batching, since activation, and periodic signals are intuitive and existing tricks, it is good to see that a combination of them can successfully train a controller.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper provides a practical guide on how to train an NN-based controller for locomotion tasks with robustness and efficiency. 

The tasks are categorized into motion primitives including running, jumping, and crawling, each with a manually designed objective function. Considering the periodic nature of locomotion, the author proposed to use sine activation layers and periodic signals in different phases as input. The experiments show that a larger batch size can lead to faster convergence. Compared to SGD, this paper points out that Adam is better for the tasks. The supplementary video presents that the trained networks enable real-time control of complex skills.

Main Review:
Based on existing differentiable physics engines, this paper further gives empirical recipes on how to use the physics gradients to train powerful controllers, which can help the community to develop techniques related to differentiable physics. 

This method divides long-horizon tasks into smaller periods with different objectives. To avoid sparse and non-smooth rewards, objective functions are manually designed for subtasks. Periodic control input is also added to the input to actuate the controller. When training the network, the authors use batching, regularization terms, and sine activation layer to accelerate and stabilize the convergence.

When reading this paper, I have several concerns,
1. In Equation 5, how is $g_v(n)$ subtracted from $A(t)$? Do they have the same dimension? $g_v(n)$ as a velocity should have a dimension of 3 (or 6 if rotation is included). The dim of  $A(t)$ should be the DOF of actuation. Maybe you mean $||g_v(n)||$. Why only target velocity is considered, how about the height $g_h(n)$? Why “periodic control signal may induce unwanted high-frequency noises”？
2. The paper mentions "a modified Adam optimizer". I did not find how Adam is modified.
3. In Figure 4, what is the definition of one iteration? Is it one step of network update or one step of simulation?

Summary Of The Review:
This paper gives us a good example of how to train a network using differentiable physics. Their video of interactively controlling 2D and 3D robots looks interesting. Although some of the techniques like staging, batching, since activation, and periodic signals are intuitive and existing tricks, it is good to see that a combination of them can successfully train a controller.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Peer Review: "Empirical Guidelines for Training Neural Network Controllers via Differentiable Physics"

**Summary:**  
This paper presents a valuable empirical framework for training robust neural network controllers using differentiable physics simulations. It effectively combines practical techniques such as periodic control signals and sine activation functions to overcome challenges in convergence. The work offers concrete recipes that could benefit the community, particularly through its task decomposition approach with manually designed objective functions and demonstration of effective strategies like batching and regularization. Real-time control capabilities shown in supplementary videos are promising. However, significant concerns regarding technical clarity and justification weaken reproducibility and impact. While the practical guidance is appreciated, the lack of novel theoretical contributions and insufficient detailing of key implementation aspects prevent a stronger endorsement.

**Strengths:**  
1.  **Practical Guidance:** The paper provides highly practical and empirically validated guidance for training robust NN controllers using differentiable physics gradients, addressing a pertinent challenge in the field.
2.  **Community Benefit:** The proposed recipes, including the task decomposition strategy with manually designed objective functions (avoiding sparse/non-smooth reward issues), periodic control inputs for enhanced actuation, and the demonstrated superiority of Adam over SGD, offer tangible benefits to researchers.
3.  **Effective Techniques:** The empirical demonstration of the effectiveness of batching and specific regularization techniques for improving convergence stability is a notable strength.
4.  **Real-World Demonstration:** The supplementary videos showcasing real-time control capabilities provide compelling evidence of the framework's practical viability and effectiveness.

**Weaknesses and Concerns:**  
1.  **Lack of Technical Clarity:** Several critical implementation details are insufficiently explained, hindering reproducibility:
    *   **Equation 5:** The dimension relationship between the actuation signal `A(t)` and the velocity targets `g_v(n)` is unclear, raising concerns about mathematical consistency and implementation. The justification for focusing *only* on velocity in this regularization term (excluding height) is also lacking.
    *   **Adam Modifications:** The specific modifications made to the Adam optimizer are underspecified. Without details, replication of the reported results (including Adam's superiority over SGD) is difficult.
    *   **Terminology Ambiguity:** The meaning of an "iteration" within the training framework (Figure 4) is ambiguous, creating confusion about the temporal granularity of the process.
2.  **Methodological Justification:** The work relies heavily on a combination of existing techniques (periodic signals, sine activations, specific regularization). While empirically shown to be effective together, the paper lacks a strong justification for *why* this specific combination is necessary or optimal beyond intuition. Theoretical grounding is minimal.
3.  **Potential Noise Issues:** The use of periodic control signals, while aiding convergence, potentially introduces high-frequency noise into the control actions. This practical concern is not addressed in the manuscript.
4.  **Limited Novelty:** The core contribution lies in the empirical combination and validation of existing tricks rather than introducing fundamentally novel theoretical concepts or algorithmic advances.

**Suggestions for Revision:**  
To strengthen the manuscript and address the concerns raised, the authors should:
1.  Provide a detailed clarification of the dimension matching and underlying rationale in **Equation 5**, explicitly explaining the relationship between `A(t)` and `g_v(n)` and justifying the focus solely on velocity.
2.  **Document Adam Modifications:** Precisely detail any changes made to the standard Adam optimizer's hyperparameters or implementation.
3.  **Define "Iteration":** Explicitly define what constitutes an "iteration" within the context of the training framework depicted in Figure 4.
4.  **Strengthen Justification:** Offer a more robust justification for the specific combination of techniques employed, moving beyond intuitive appeal to explain why this ensemble is particularly effective for differentiable physics-based controller training.
5.  **Address Noise Concerns:** Discuss the potential for high-frequency noise induced by periodic control signals and whether any mitigation strategies were employed or are needed.

**Overall Recommendation and Decision:**  
This work offers valuable empirical insights and practical guidelines for a relevant problem in learning-based control. The demonstration of functional real-time controllers is commendable. However, the significant technical ambiguities (especially regarding Equation 5, Adam modifications, and terminology) substantially weaken the paper's reproducibility and impact. The reliance on established techniques without deeper theoretical justification also limits the perceived novelty. Addressing the major clarity issues (points 1-3 in Suggestions) is crucial. While the practical utility provides merit, the current presentation flaws prevent a higher rating. **I recommend acceptance, but only marginally above the threshold, contingent upon satisfactory revision of the critical clarity issues highlighted.**

Detail modifications made to Adam optimizer
Explicitly define what constitutes an 'iteration' in training framework
Provide stronger justification for combination of existing techniques
Address high-frequency noise concerns from periodic control signals

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes to use differentiable simulators for learning locomotion skills for deformable characters. The key contribution is the experiments and analysis of a large number of design choices, including activation function, reward design, optimizer, batch size, etc. The paper finds that by combining differentiable simulator with the right design choices, the character can learn general and complex skills with one policy. The paper also shows that this combination can significantly outperform PPO.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:
1) This paper combines differentiable simulation with many carefully chosen design decisions, which enables effective learning of soft body locomotion.
2) The analysis and ablation study provides useful and practical guidance to practitioners in the field of reinforcement learning and locomotion control.

Weakness:
1) Technical contribution is lean. The Taichi differentiable simulator is an existing work. This paper seems to use it without lots of changes. The second claimed contribution "end-to-end differentiable simulation environment, including mass-spring and material point" is not well supported. The description of simulation is high level or in Section 2. It is hard to understand the scope of this contribution.

2) I hope that the paper can demonstrate more impressive and diverse results. For example, a soft character navigating uneven terrains, or a soft robot manipulating objects. A soft body character running on flat ground, as shown in this paper, has been demonstrated in several prior works, such as DiffTaichi. Even though this paper demonstrates multiple locomotion skills (turning, different speed and jumping) in one policy, the improvement over the prior work is incremental.

Additional questions and comments:
1) The paper claims in Section 2.1 that "Any physically-based deformable object simulator can be applied to our learning framework". I thought that the simulator needs to be differentiable. So only a small subset of simulation will work. Did I miss anything?

2) It is unclear to me how applicable the results of the ablation study are to other tasks. For example, the sine actuation function and the periodic signal may not be as important for manipulation tasks. If the application of this paper is only locomotion, its contributions are quite limited.

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes to use differentiable simulators for learning locomotion skills for deformable characters. The key contribution is the experiments and analysis of a large number of design choices, including activation function, reward design, optimizer, batch size, etc. The paper finds that by combining differentiable simulator with the right design choices, the character can learn general and complex skills with one policy. The paper also shows that this combination can significantly outperform PPO.

Main Review:
Strengths:
1) This paper combines differentiable simulation with many carefully chosen design decisions, which enables effective learning of soft body locomotion.
2) The analysis and ablation study provides useful and practical guidance to practitioners in the field of reinforcement learning and locomotion control.

Weakness:
1) Technical contribution is lean. The Taichi differentiable simulator is an existing work. This paper seems to use it without lots of changes. The second claimed contribution "end-to-end differentiable simulation environment, including mass-spring and material point" is not well supported. The description of simulation is high level or in Section 2. It is hard to understand the scope of this contribution.

2) I hope that the paper can demonstrate more impressive and diverse results. For example, a soft character navigating uneven terrains, or a soft robot manipulating objects. A soft body character running on flat ground, as shown in this paper, has been demonstrated in several prior works, such as DiffTaichi. Even though this paper demonstrates multiple locomotion skills (turning, different speed and jumping) in one policy, the improvement over the prior work is incremental.

Additional questions and comments:
1) The paper claims in Section 2.1 that "Any physically-based deformable object simulator can be applied to our learning framework". I thought that the simulator needs to be differentiable. So only a small subset of simulation will work. Did I miss anything?

2) It is unclear to me how applicable the results of the ablation study are to other tasks. For example, the sine actuation function and the periodic signal may not be as important for manipulation tasks. If the application of this paper is only locomotion, its contributions are quite limited.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper demonstrates good results for learning soft body locomotion. Although the technical novelty is lean, I find that the analysis and ablation of various design decisions are helpful, and has practical values to guide further development in this direction. For this reason, I am slightly leaning towards accepting the paper.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Peer Review: "Learning Soft Body Locomotion with Differentiable Simulation"  

**Overall Summary:**  
This paper presents a method for learning soft body locomotion by combining differentiable simulation with specific design choices. While the approach demonstrates effective results and offers practical insights through its ablation study, the technical contribution is incremental. The work marginally exceeds the acceptance threshold due to its empirical utility, but key limitations—particularly regarding novelty and scope—prevent a higher rating.  

**Strengths:**  
1. **Effective Integration of Techniques:** The combination of differentiable simulation (using Taichi) with carefully selected design elements (e.g., periodic signals, sine activations) enables efficient learning of complex locomotion skills. This synergy is well-executed and practically valuable.  
2. **Practical Ablation Study:** The analysis of batch size, optimizer choices (modified Adam), and loss functions provides actionable guidance for practitioners in reinforcement learning and locomotion control. The identification of critical components (e.g., periodic signals) is particularly useful.  

**Weaknesses:**  
1. **Limited Technical Novelty:** The core technical contribution relies heavily on the existing Taichi differentiable simulator without significant modifications or extensions. This results in an incremental advance over prior work (e.g., DiffTaichi), despite the inclusion of multiple locomotion skills in a single policy.  
2. **Narrow Scope of Results:** The demonstrated tasks lack diversity and ambition. For instance, the absence of challenges like uneven terrain navigation or object manipulation fails to showcase broader applicability or surpass prior art convincingly.  
3. **Unsubstantiated Claims:** The assertion that the method works with "any physically-based deformable simulator" is unsupported, as the approach fundamentally requires *differentiable* simulators. Additionally, the claimed contribution of an "end-to-end differentiable simulation environment" lacks clear technical justification.  

**Suggestions for Improvement:**  
1. **Expand Task Complexity:** Demonstrate more diverse and challenging scenarios (e.g., locomotion on uneven terrains or soft-body object manipulation) to better validate the method’s robustness and distinguish it from existing work.  
2. **Clarify Applicability:** Discuss whether findings from the ablation study (e.g., optimizer choices, periodic signals) generalize to non-locomotion tasks (e.g., manipulation) to broaden the work’s impact.  
3. **Refine Claims:** (a) Revise the claim that "any" deformable simulator is compatible—only differentiable simulators are applicable. (b) Strengthen the justification for the "differentiable simulation environment" contribution, either through technical innovation or rigorous comparison.  

**Recommendation:**  
**Rating: 6 (Marginally above acceptance threshold)**  
This paper offers practical value through its empirical framework and ablation insights, warranting acceptance. However, its limited novelty, narrow task scope, and unsupported claims constrain its impact. The authors should address the above weaknesses in revision to solidify the contribution.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes to learn neural network controllers in a differentiable simulator for locomotion tasks. A set of technical improvements are made and make the controllers more robust and require fewer samplers to learn. These include: using periodic activation functions, design of loss functions, and using large batch size. The paper shows learned locomotion skills of running, jumping, and crawling for different soft robots, which can be interactively controlled.
reviews.main_review:
### Strength
* +++ The paper shows great results of interactively controlling soft robot locomotion and switching among different goals.

### Weakness
* --- The overall technical contribution is limited. The proposed contribution on loss designs is limited to the locomotion tasks, and it is not clear whether these losses would generalize to other robots and the real world [1, 2].
* -- The use of the sine activation function is not well supported by the experiments. In table 1, sine function shows marginal improvement over tanh, and in table 5 of the appendix, using tanh shows much better performance over sine. Furthermore, tanh tends to become saturated during backpropagation of a long sequence. I would suggest the author also compares with ReLU.
* -- Presentation in the paper can be improved:
     1. The modified Adam optimizer is listed as one of the technical contributions. However, it is not stated clearly what is the modification and how does it compare to the original Adam optimizer. 
     2. The name of the robots are presented in Fig. 7 of Sec. 4.5 but are referenced multiple times in previous sections and table 1. It would be helpful to clarify the name of the agents early on. 
     3. Reference of Fig. 5 at the end of Sec. 4.2 seems to be incorrect, as no comparison of SGD and Adam is shown in Fig. 5.

[1] Lee, Joonho, et al. "Learning quadrupedal locomotion over challenging terrain." Science robotics 5.47 (2020).
[2] Zhao, Allan, et al. "RoboGrammar: graph grammar for terrain-optimized robot design." ACM Transactions on Graphics (TOG) 39.6 (2020): 1-16.

Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes to learn neural network controllers in a differentiable simulator for locomotion tasks. A set of technical improvements are made and make the controllers more robust and require fewer samplers to learn. These include: using periodic activation functions, design of loss functions, and using large batch size. The paper shows learned locomotion skills of running, jumping, and crawling for different soft robots, which can be interactively controlled.

Main Review:
### Strength
* +++ The paper shows great results of interactively controlling soft robot locomotion and switching among different goals.

### Weakness
* --- The overall technical contribution is limited. The proposed contribution on loss designs is limited to the locomotion tasks, and it is not clear whether these losses would generalize to other robots and the real world [1, 2].
* -- The use of the sine activation function is not well supported by the experiments. In table 1, sine function shows marginal improvement over tanh, and in table 5 of the appendix, using tanh shows much better performance over sine. Furthermore, tanh tends to become saturated during backpropagation of a long sequence. I would suggest the author also compares with ReLU.
* -- Presentation in the paper can be improved:
     1. The modified Adam optimizer is listed as one of the technical contributions. However, it is not stated clearly what is the modification and how does it compare to the original Adam optimizer. 
     2. The name of the robots are presented in Fig. 7 of Sec. 4.5 but are referenced multiple times in previous sections and table 1. It would be helpful to clarify the name of the agents early on. 
     3. Reference of Fig. 5 at the end of Sec. 4.2 seems to be incorrect, as no comparison of SGD and Adam is shown in Fig. 5.

[1] Lee, Joonho, et al. "Learning quadrupedal locomotion over challenging terrain." Science robotics 5.47 (2020).
[2] Zhao, Allan, et al. "RoboGrammar: graph grammar for terrain-optimized robot design." ACM Transactions on Graphics (TOG) 39.6 (2020): 1-16.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper shows interesting results of locomotion tasks, but the overall technical contribution is limited as presented. The writing of the paper could also be further improved. I am willing to raise my score if the listed concerns can be addressed.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Peer Review: "Differentiable Physics-Based Learning for Complex Locomotion Skills in Soft Robots"  

**Overall Summary:**  
This paper introduces a differentiable physics-based learning framework for interactive control of soft robots, demonstrating impressive locomotion skills and goal-switching capabilities. While the results are visually compelling, the technical contributions are limited in novelty and generalizability. Significant presentation issues further undermine the work's clarity. The core methodology appears to be an incremental extension of existing techniques without robust validation of its broader applicability.  

**Strengths:**  
- The framework successfully achieves **interactive control of complex soft robot locomotion**, enabling dynamic switching among multiple goals (e.g., walking, jumping). The visual results are compelling and highlight the potential for real-time applications.  

**Weaknesses:**  
1. **Limited Technical Contribution:**  
   The loss functions and control strategies are highly task-specific, with no evidence of generalizability to other robot morphologies or real-world scenarios. The paper does not establish how the proposed approach advances beyond existing differentiable physics or reinforcement learning methods.  

2. **Insufficient Validation of Sine Activation:**  
   The claimed superiority of sine over tanh activations is weakly supported. While Table 1 shows marginal gains for sine in some tasks, Appendix Table 5 reveals that **tanh outperforms sine in key experiments**. The hypothesis that tanh suffers from gradient saturation in long sequences lacks empirical validation. No comparison with ReLU—a standard baseline—is provided.  

3. **Critical Presentation Flaws:**  
   - The **modified Adam optimizer** is ambiguously described, with no clear explanation of how it differs from the standard implementation.  
   - **Agent names (e.g., "Alpaca," "Monster")** are referenced in early sections but never defined, causing confusion.  
   - **Figure 5 is incorrectly cited** as containing the SGD vs. Adam comparison, which it does not. This erodes confidence in the analysis.  

**Suggestions for Improvement:**  
1. **Methodology:**  
   - Compare sine activations with **ReLU** to strengthen claims.  
   - Clarify the **Adam optimizer modifications** with explicit algorithmic details or pseudocode.  
   - Discuss the framework’s limitations regarding generalizability to non-locomotion tasks or physical hardware.  

2. **Presentation:**  
   - **Define all agent names** upon first use (e.g., in Section 3 or Figure captions).  
   - **Correct the figure reference** for the SGD vs. Adam results (currently misattributed to Figure 5).  
   - Reconcile the contradictory results between Table 1 and Appendix Table 5 regarding sine/tanh performance.  

**Decision:**  
**Rating: 5 (Marginally below acceptance threshold)**  
While the interactive control results are visually striking, the work’s technical contributions are incremental and lack rigorous validation. The inconsistent evidence for sine activations and significant presentation issues further diminish its impact. Substantial revisions—particularly in generalizability analysis and clarity—are needed to meet the acceptance threshold. I recommend rejection in its current form but encourage resubmission after addressing these concerns.

title: On the Unreasonable Effectiveness of Feature Propagation in Learning on Graphs with Missing Node Features

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors consider the case of training GNNs with missing features. The proposed framework consists of a diffusion based step prior to training the GNN. A discretization of the approach leads to what they term "feature propagation", a scalable method to impute features on the graph. The assumption is that the energy function that determines how related the features are to each other can be learnt from the data. The authors consider the gradient flow and it's solution to minimize the dirichlet energy, which is far more scalable than computing the closed form solution. Experiments on multiple datasets show that the proposed method outperforms several baselines.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:

The paper is well written, and easy to follow. The problem the authors address is interesting, and the solution provided is simple, yet elegant. The experimental results are thorough, both in terms of comparing performance and speed of the method. 

Weaknesses:

It's not completely clear how the proposed method extends to vector valued features. Is the process repeated independently for each feature? or is there some "interdependencies" between feature dimensions that can be made use of? How does that affect the computational complexity?
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
Initial assessment : accept

The paper is well written, and easy to follow. The problem the authors address is interesting, and the solution provided is simple, yet elegant. The experimental results are thorough, both in terms of comparing performance and speed of the method. Missing value imputation is a problem that is often ill-addressed when it comes to ML research. In this paper, the authors specifically look at imputation in the context of training GNNs, and use the graph to perform the imputation. 

I feel the authors need to address the difference between the proposed method and label propagation a bit more. They claim it's different because of labels being discrete and the diffusion operator being different, but I'm not clear why that's a deal breaker. Won't the algorithm be the same but with a new operator? 

Another point to address is the extension to vector valued features. They claim the extension is straightforward, but a note to this effect will be nice. 

other comments:
P1 last paragraph : I’m not sure if being unaware to the task is a bad thing necessarily.  isn't it better to do imputation in a task agnostic fashion?

Fig 3: Please make the text larger. The figures are hard to read. 

Fig 3: Also any intuition why Random is so much worse than Zero in all these datasets?

reviews.text:
## Peer Review: Recommendation and Comments  

**Overall Recommendation:**  
**8: Accept (Good Paper)**  
This paper presents a valuable contribution and is recommended for acceptance. However, minor revisions are required to address clarity issues before final publication.  

### Strengths:  
1.  **Clarity and Writing:** The paper is exceptionally well-written, logically structured, and easy to follow.  
2.  **Problem Significance:** It addresses an important and timely problem – the imputation of missing features in Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) – which has significant practical implications.  
3.  **Solution Elegance:** The proposed solution is both simple and elegant, offering a compelling approach to feature imputation.  
4.  **Experimental Rigor:** The experimental evaluation is thorough and convincing, providing comprehensive comparisons of both performance and computational speed against relevant baselines.  

### Weaknesses and Areas Requiring Clarification:  
While the core contribution is strong, two key technical aspects require clearer exposition:  
1.  **Handling Vector-Valued Features:** The extension of the method to vector-valued features lacks sufficient detail. It is unclear whether features across different dimensions are processed independently or if interdependencies are considered. The implications of this choice and its impact on the method's validity need explicit discussion.  
2.  **Computational Complexity for Vector-Valued Features:** Relatedly, the computational complexity implications of handling vector-valued features are not addressed. A brief analysis of how complexity scales with feature dimensionality would strengthen the technical foundation.  

### Suggestions for Revision:  
To improve clarity and completeness, please address the following points in the revision:  
1.  **Feature Propagation vs. Label Propagation:** Provide a more thorough discussion distinguishing Feature Propagation (FP) from Label Propagation (LP). Specifically, elaborate on why discrete labels (as handled by LP) are described as a "deal-breaker" for direct application of the FP approach. Could a modified operator reconcile these scenarios?  
2.  **Clarify Vector-Valued Feature Handling:** Explicitly clarify how vector-valued features are processed (e.g., independently per dimension or with cross-dimensional dependencies) in the methodology section. Even if the approach is straightforward, stating it clearly is essential.  
3.  **Figure 3 Legibility:** Increase the text size (axis labels, legend, annotations) in Figure 3 to ensure readability.  
4.  **Baseline Performance Intuition:** Offer an explanation or intuition for why the "Random" imputation baseline performs worse than "Zero" imputation in the experimental results. Understanding this behavior is important for interpreting the baselines.  

### Summary:  
This paper tackles an interesting problem with an elegant solution, backed by solid experimental validation. The core strengths in writing, problem selection, solution design, and experimentation make it a strong candidate for acceptance. The requested revisions focus primarily on clarifying technical details regarding the handling of vector-valued features and the distinction from Label Propagation, along with minor presentational improvements. Addressing these points will enhance the paper's clarity and impact.  

**Decision:** **Accept (Good Paper) - Minor Revisions Required**
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors consider the case of training GNNs with missing features. The proposed framework consists of a diffusion based step prior to training the GNN. A discretization of the approach leads to what they term "feature propagation", a scalable method to impute features on the graph. The assumption is that the energy function that determines how related the features are to each other can be learnt from the data. The authors consider the gradient flow and it's solution to minimize the dirichlet energy, which is far more scalable than computing the closed form solution. Experiments on multiple datasets show that the proposed method outperforms several baselines.

Main Review:
Strengths:

The paper is well written, and easy to follow. The problem the authors address is interesting, and the solution provided is simple, yet elegant. The experimental results are thorough, both in terms of comparing performance and speed of the method. 

Weaknesses:

It's not completely clear how the proposed method extends to vector valued features. Is the process repeated independently for each feature? or is there some "interdependencies" between feature dimensions that can be made use of? How does that affect the computational complexity?

Summary Of The Review:
Initial assessment : accept

The paper is well written, and easy to follow. The problem the authors address is interesting, and the solution provided is simple, yet elegant. The experimental results are thorough, both in terms of comparing performance and speed of the method. Missing value imputation is a problem that is often ill-addressed when it comes to ML research. In this paper, the authors specifically look at imputation in the context of training GNNs, and use the graph to perform the imputation. 

I feel the authors need to address the difference between the proposed method and label propagation a bit more. They claim it's different because of labels being discrete and the diffusion operator being different, but I'm not clear why that's a deal breaker. Won't the algorithm be the same but with a new operator? 

Another point to address is the extension to vector valued features. They claim the extension is straightforward, but a note to this effect will be nice. 

other comments:
P1 last paragraph : I’m not sure if being unaware to the task is a bad thing necessarily.  isn't it better to do imputation in a task agnostic fashion?

Fig 3: Please make the text larger. The figures are hard to read. 

Fig 3: Also any intuition why Random is so much worse than Zero in all these datasets?

Rating:
8: accept, good paper

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: A graph neural network (GNN) pre-processing step for completing missing node features is presented: feature propagation (FP). The method is based on minimisation of Dirichlet energy. A more efficient iterative update aproach is derived avoiding matrix inversion (cubic operation) to a series of sparse-to-dense matrix multiplications. The method acts similar to a low-pass filter for missing features, which complements the GNN learning algorithm. Relevant work is covered and similarities and differences mentioned in many cases. Experiments on seven publicly available datasets show that the method is effective and performs especially well when a large proportion of the feature values are missing (above 90%). A run-time analysis of wall-clock performance compares their method to two state-of-the-art methods for handling missing node features in GNNs and their method is 3 x faster in these comparison. A weakness of their method, FP, is analysed; FP assumes the graphs are homophlic, that nearby nodes have similar node features but if this is not the case then the method does not perform well. This is some interesting future work.
reviews.main_review:
This paper addresses a very interesting problem that is relevant to real-world applications; dealing with missing node features for graph neural networks (GNNs). The approach is based on minimising Dirichlet energy, which is a emthod previously used for graph regularisation and has relations with graph Laplcians, so is well suited to working with data on graph topology. The author(s) derive an efficient iterative algorithm which makes the approach scalable to large graphs which makes it more practical for real-world applications. Many public datasets were used for benchmarking that show very promising results.

In the experiments, it's surprising how well the nearest-neigbour averaging worked. In the comparison method's own papers SAT paper has a 'NeighAggre' baseline that seems to do something similar but gets worse scores than their method and GCNMF compares with kNN with k=5, and this also performs worse than their method. Do you have some insights into why neighbourhood averaging performs so well?

This method is a pre-processing step, not integrated into the GNN, as such many other methods for estimating the missing values could be used. One justification of this choice of approach is that is acts as a low-pass filter, and the GNN mainly learns from the lower frequency components.  This work is very close to matrix completion with graph side information.In this domain there are other methods that are highly scalable and are able to accurately estimate missing values when large amounts of the data (>99%) are missing [1, 2, 4]. Furthermore, one method uses a similar optimisation approach of iterative dense-sparse matrix mulitplication [1]. Worth note is that an extension to [1] will also remove edges in the graph that do not have high-homophily that could improve results for low-himopholy results. Also [3] is a kernel method for matrix completion that allows for a choice of covariance functions, including a diffusion kernel that is therefore similar to this approach. I think it is worth at mentioning this in comparison to this approach and even adding emprical evaluation against these methods, especially [1].

Other small comments:

You report using V100 GPUs (16 or 32Gb?), how much memory did GCNMF take before ran out of memory.

It wasn't crystal clear how the approach works for vector node attributes, do you simply repeat the algorithm for each dimension separately?

"Proposition 1: inverse of Laplacian depends on a connected graph, as eigen values are 0 if graph has clusters." How true is this assumption in real graphs, and what can be done in the case where a graph is not connected?

How do you know 40 iterations is enough, is there a easy guide to this and what happens if you keep running the algorithm, will it eventually make all missing featues almost the same if run for a large number of iterations?

"For a fair comparison, we use the same standard hyperparameters for all methods across all experiments." Does this mean that the downstream 2-layer GCN model has the same hyperparameters? 

Edits:
    A_u should be A_uu ?
    
    Zhu and Ghahramani reference is incomplete

    donwstream task -> downstream task


1. Rao, Nikhil, et al. "Collaborative Filtering with Graph Information: Consistency and Scalable Methods." NIPS. Vol. 2. No. 4. 2015.

2. Strahl, Jonathan, et al. "Scalable probabilistic matrix factorization with graph-based priors." Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence. Vol. 34. No. 04. 2020.

3. Zhou, Tinghui, et al. "Kernelized probabilistic matrix factorization: Exploiting graphs and side information." Proceedings of the 2012 SIAM international Conference on Data mining. Society for Industrial and Applied Mathematics, 2012.

4. Monti, Federico, Michael M. Bronstein, and Xavier Bresson. "Geometric matrix completion with recurrent multi-graph neural networks." arXiv preprint arXiv:1704.06803 (2017).
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
As this is a preprocessing step that essentially is similar to matrix completion with graph side information, then I believe that comparison with this literature is missing. Otherwise this is a very good paper. The approach they have for the problem, which could be seen as matrix completion with graph side information, is I believe novel in that domain, as well as in this framework for graph neural networks. Therefore, comparison with this domain is important here, as other methods in this domain could also be well justified as pre-processing steps. One such method that both scalable (using similar final equation, but dervied from a different approach, conjugate gradients), must be compared both in the paper and experiments in my opinion. Otherwise, it feels like the work is trying to avoid comparison with similar methods by claiming this work is related to GNN, while really it feels like this is a matrix completion with graph side information method, that is then used for GNNs.

After feedback:

I still feel that the motivation for using this particular method of estimating the missing values is not clearly better than other approaches. Also, the link to GNN seems weak as essentially this is a method for completing missing data and with this seems better suited to the domain for estimating missing values, where a stronger comparison with other missing values estimating methods can be compared - for example in comparison to recommender system approaches like collaborative filtering with graph side information. The link to GNN could be a small subsection in that style of paper. I don't see why running GNN is such a large part of this work when it's a stand alone preprocessing step. Therefore I've lowered my score from 6 to 5.

reviews.text:
## Peer Review: "Feature Propagation on Graph Neural Networks with Missing Node Features"

**Overall Assessment**  
This paper presents a theoretically motivated Feature Propagation (FP) method for handling missing node features in graph neural networks, demonstrating efficiency and strong performance at high missing-feature rates. While the approach is novel and addresses a practical problem, significant limitations—particularly the reliance on graph homophily, insufficient contextualization within matrix completion literature, and underdeveloped justification as a standalone pre-process—prevent it from reaching the acceptance threshold. I recommend **revision with major improvements** (rating: **5 - marginally below acceptance threshold**).

### Strengths  
1.  **Problem Relevance:** The work tackles a critical real-world challenge in GNN applications: robust handling of missing node features.  
2.  **Theoretical Foundation:** The connection to Dirichlet energy minimization provides a solid theoretical motivation for the proposed FP algorithm.  
3.  **Efficiency & Scalability:** The iterative algorithm is a significant practical strength, efficiently replacing costly matrix inversion with sparse-to-dense operations. Reported runtimes (~10s for OGBN-Products on a single GPU) and a 3x speed advantage over GCNMF/PaGNN are compelling.  
4.  **High Missing-Rate Performance:** Empirical results convincingly show FP outperforms baselines, particularly at very high missing-feature rates (>90%).  

### Weaknesses and Major Concerns  
1.  **Homophily Dependence:** The critical assumption of graph homophily is a fundamental limitation. The significant performance degradation on non-homophilic graphs severely restricts the method's general applicability and requires explicit acknowledgment as a key weakness needing future work.  
2.  **Inadequate Matrix Completion Context:** Framing FP as matrix completion with graph side information is appropriate, yet the omission of comparisons with scalable matrix completion methods (e.g., Rao et al. 2015, Monti et al.) undermines claims of novelty and scalability. This gap is a major oversight.  
3.  **Weak Standalone Justification:** The paper's positioning of FP as a standalone pre-processing step is underdeveloped. The link to downstream GNN performance and integration within the GNN literature feels tenuous, weakening the core contribution narrative.  
4.  **Baseline Analysis Insufficiency:** The strong performance of simple baselines like neighbor averaging warrants deeper analysis. The paper lacks insights into *why* these simple methods perform well, limiting understanding of FP's relative advantages.  

### Suggestions for Improvement  
*   **Empirical Comparisons:** Conduct thorough comparisons with scalable matrix completion baselines, especially Rao et al. (2015), to properly situate FP's novelty and performance.  
*   **Deepen Baseline Analysis:** Provide analysis explaining the strong performance of simple neighbor-averaging baselines.  
*   **Technical Clarifications:**  
    *   Explicitly state and justify the handling of vector-valued features via independent per-channel diffusion.  
    *   Address the implications of Proposition 1's connected-graph assumption for real-world disconnected graphs.  
    *   Discuss convergence behavior and justify why 40 iterations suffices across datasets.  
*   **Methodological Rigor:**  
    *   Verify and ensure consistent hyperparameters were used for all downstream GCN models in comparative evaluations.  
    *   Report the memory usage of GCNMF leading to Out-of-Memory (OOM) errors for transparency.  
*   **Presentation & Detail:**  
    *   Fix identified typos (e.g., 'A_u' → 'A_uu', 'donwstream' → 'downstream').  
    *   Complete the Zhu & Ghahramani reference.  
    *   Expand the discussion on FP acting as a low-pass filter and its alignment with GNNs' use of low-frequency components.  

### Conclusion  
While the proposed Feature Propagation method is efficient, scalable, and effective for homophilic graphs with high rates of missing features, the paper's current form has significant shortcomings. The lack of comparison with relevant matrix completion literature, the critical dependence on homophily, and the underdeveloped justification for FP as a standalone GNN contribution substantially weaken the work. Addressing the major concerns and suggestions outlined above is essential for establishing the paper's contribution and suitability for publication. In its present state, it falls marginally below the acceptance threshold.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes a fast way to do node feature imputation in graph learning settings. In a nutshell, the idea is to consider the graph topology in the imputation by doing a feature by feature minimization of the Laplacian quadratic form. However, since the exact solution to this requires a matrix inversion, this quantity is minimized by a gradient descent procedure that relies on sparse matrix multiplications.
reviews.main_review:
Through numerical experiments the authors illustrate the benefit of this simple approach. Nonetheless, my main concern has to do with the novelty of this. Using the quadratic form of the graph Laplacian (Dirichlet energy) for label propagation or graph signal interpolation has been used several times over the last decade (as referenced in this paper). Moreover, using a gradient descent approach instead of inverting the Laplacian has also been used quite often. E.g., see the tutorial "Signal processing on higher-order networks: Livin’on the edge... and beyond" where in Section 2.4.2 the authors review some typical formulations of signals on graphs before going into higher-order networks. Notice that Equation (4) contains (I-L)^k, which corresponds to several applications of the (normalized) adjacency matrix. In fact, it is mentioned that "This may be interpreted in terms of k gradient descent steps of the cost function (Dirichlet energy)".

From the perspective of graph signal processing, the true solution with the inverse of the Laplacian can be seen as an IIR filter and this is an FIR approximation of the filter. This idea of graph filter design has also been studied quite a bit over the last 7 years.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Under this perspective, I regard that the novelty in this paper is limited.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes a fast way to do node feature imputation in graph learning settings. In a nutshell, the idea is to consider the graph topology in the imputation by doing a feature by feature minimization of the Laplacian quadratic form. However, since the exact solution to this requires a matrix inversion, this quantity is minimized by a gradient descent procedure that relies on sparse matrix multiplications.

Main Review:
Through numerical experiments the authors illustrate the benefit of this simple approach. Nonetheless, my main concern has to do with the novelty of this. Using the quadratic form of the graph Laplacian (Dirichlet energy) for label propagation or graph signal interpolation has been used several times over the last decade (as referenced in this paper). Moreover, using a gradient descent approach instead of inverting the Laplacian has also been used quite often. E.g., see the tutorial "Signal processing on higher-order networks: Livin’on the edge... and beyond" where in Section 2.4.2 the authors review some typical formulations of signals on graphs before going into higher-order networks. Notice that Equation (4) contains (I-L)^k, which corresponds to several applications of the (normalized) adjacency matrix. In fact, it is mentioned that "This may be interpreted in terms of k gradient descent steps of the cost function (Dirichlet energy)".

From the perspective of graph signal processing, the true solution with the inverse of the Laplacian can be seen as an IIR filter and this is an FIR approximation of the filter. This idea of graph filter design has also been studied quite a bit over the last 7 years.

Summary Of The Review:
Under this perspective, I regard that the novelty in this paper is limited.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Peer Review  

**Summary**  
This paper proposes a fast method for node feature imputation by minimizing Dirichlet energy via gradient descent with sparse matrix operations, avoiding explicit matrix inversion. While the approach demonstrates computational efficiency and empirical benefits, the core idea aligns closely with established techniques in graph signal processing, raising concerns about its novelty.  

**Strengths**  
1. **Computational Efficiency**: The method leverages gradient descent with sparse matrix multiplications, offering a scalable solution for node feature imputation in large graphs.  
2. **Empirical Validation**: Numerical experiments convincingly demonstrate the practical benefits of the approach, including competitive imputation accuracy and reduced runtime.  

**Weaknesses**  
1. **Limited Novelty**: The core technical contribution—minimizing Dirichlet energy (a Laplacian quadratic form) via gradient descent for graph signal interpolation—is not novel. This framework is well-established in prior literature (e.g., label propagation and graph signal processing).  
2. **Overlap with FIR Approximations**: The proposed iterative scheme (interpreted as repeated applications of the adjacency matrix, e.g., \((I - L)^k\)) functionally resembles existing Finite Impulse Response (FIR) approximations for graph filters. Such approximations have been extensively studied for efficient graph signal interpolation, diminishing the originality of the current work.  
3. **Gradient Descent as a Known Optimization Strategy**: Replacing exact matrix inversion (which yields an Infinite Impulse Response/IIR filter) with gradient descent is a recognized optimization strategy in prior art. The paper does not sufficiently differentiate this aspect from existing methodologies.  

**Technical Assessment**  
The method minimizes Dirichlet energy iteratively via sparse gradient steps (Equation 4), which approximates the solution typically obtained by Laplacian matrix inversion. While efficient, this iterative scheme is equivalent to an FIR filter approximation of the ideal IIR filter (the exact solution). This connection to FIR approximations in graph signal processing is well-documented, and the paper does not adequately address how its approach fundamentally advances beyond this established paradigm.  

**Recommendation**  
While the paper presents a practical and well-executed method for fast feature imputation, the limited conceptual novelty relative to prior work in graph signal processing is a significant drawback. The core technique—iterative energy minimization via gradient descent—builds directly on existing FIR filter approximations and optimization strategies without introducing a substantively new algorithmic insight. The empirical results, though solid, do not compensate for this lack of originality.  

**Decision**: **Reject (Marginally Below Acceptance Threshold)**  
The work is technically sound and practically useful but does not meet the novelty bar for acceptance, as it primarily reframes existing concepts without significant innovation.

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes a fast way to do node feature imputation in graph learning settings. In a nutshell, the idea is to consider the graph topology in the imputation by doing a feature by feature minimization of the Laplacian quadratic form. However, since the exact solution to this requires a matrix inversion, this quantity is minimized by a gradient descent procedure that relies on sparse matrix multiplications.

Main Review:
Through numerical experiments the authors illustrate the benefit of this simple approach. Nonetheless, my main concern has to do with the novelty of this. Using the quadratic form of the graph Laplacian (Dirichlet energy) for label propagation or graph signal interpolation has been used several times over the last decade (as referenced in this paper). Moreover, using a gradient descent approach instead of inverting the Laplacian has also been used quite often. E.g., see the tutorial "Signal processing on higher-order networks: Livin’on the edge... and beyond" where in Section 2.4.2 the authors review some typical formulations of signals on graphs before going into higher-order networks. Notice that Equation (4) contains (I-L)^k, which corresponds to several applications of the (normalized) adjacency matrix. In fact, it is mentioned that "This may be interpreted in terms of k gradient descent steps of the cost function (Dirichlet energy)".

From the perspective of graph signal processing, the true solution with the inverse of the Laplacian can be seen as an IIR filter and this is an FIR approximation of the filter. This idea of graph filter design has also been studied quite a bit over the last 7 years.

Summary Of The Review:
Under this perspective, I regard that the novelty in this paper is limited.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper address the problem of  missing node features in graph by conducting feature propagation (FP). An iterative updated algorithm is presented. FP is a preprocessing step and can be used in multiple downstream tasks. Experiments on node classification shows the effectiveness of FP. The authors also provided comparison on running time and showed how homophily affects the performance of FP.
reviews.main_review:
The strengths:

1. The paper is clearly written and easy to follow
2. The performance on missing 99% of the features is impressive
3. The speed improvement is promising

The weaknesses:

1. FP is not a new idea/algorithm, it is essentially same as label propagation (LP) by Zhu et al. [1]. Although the authors argued that "Differently from our setting of diffusion of continuous node features, they deal with discrete label classes directly, resulting in a different diffusion operator.", from my understanding, there is not substantial difference. LP also propagate the label probability, which is continuous. The derivation, the update formula of FP are the same as LP, the only difference is to substitute the weight matrix in LP with Laplacian matrix. Interpreting label propagation via Dirchlet energy is also proposed in [2], where instead of using $x_i$, $x_j$, Solomon et al. were using $f_i$, $f_j$, which are label function of node $i$ and $j$. Consider $x_i$'s as the soft label with label function $f_i$, then they are essentially the same thing.
2. The experiment part shows that simple averaging of the neighbors gives good result when the missing portion is $\leq$ 50%. In that case, I think it is also important to show experimental results of algorithms that do not use node features, to see if there is really a gap on the performance. (Note that under strong assumption of homophily, it is not surprising that simple averaging of neighbors works well. FP or LP takes account the topology of the graph and changes simple averaging of neighbors to weighted average of the whole network. The global information of the graph is the key to a better performance in this case, and I doubt other algorithms using the global information can also get similar good performance.) For example, one can use simple label propagation on the node classes, or using LINE, 
DeepWalk or node2vec to get a embedding of each node and then classifying with the embeddings. If the performance is similar as what FP gives when there are 99% features missing, I would argue that in that case we can simply go with classical algorithms using only the network topology. We will only use GNN when we have enough features available, while in which case it seems simple averaging of neighbors is good enough. The paper is not so convincing at the current stage.

[1] Xiaojin, Zhu, and Ghahramani Zoubin. "Learning from labeled and unlabeled data with label propagation." Tech. Rep., Technical Report CMU-CALD-02–107, Carnegie Mellon University (2002).

[2] Solomon, Justin, Raif Rustamov, Leonidas Guibas, and Adrian Butscher. "Wasserstein propagation for semi-supervised learning." In International Conference on Machine Learning, pp. 306-314. PMLR, 2014.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: In general I like the idea of using FP as a preprocessing step. However, I am not convinced that the idea is novel enough to be accepted in ICLR, details are referred to my main review.

The weaknesses:

Interpreting label propagation via Dirchlet energy is also proposed in [2], where instead of using $x_i$, $x_j$, Solomon et al. were using $f_i$, $f_j$, which are label function of node $i$ and $j$. DeepWalk or node2vec to get a embedding of each node and then classifying with the embeddings. We will only use GNN when we have enough features available, while in which case it seems simple averaging of neighbors is good enough. The paper is not so convincing at the current stage.

[1] Xiaojin, Zhu, and Ghahramani Zoubin. "Learning from labeled and unlabeled data with label propagation." Tech. Rep., Technical Report CMU-CALD-02–107, Carnegie Mellon University (2002).

reviews.text:
## Peer Review: Feature Propagation for Handling Missing Node Features  

**Overall Summary:**  
This paper introduces Feature Propagation (FP), an iterative algorithm for imputing missing node features in graph neural networks. The method demonstrates strong performance under extreme missing feature rates (e.g., 99%) and exhibits computational efficiency. However, significant concerns regarding novelty and experimental validation limit the contribution. The core methodology is strikingly similar to Label Propagation (LP), and the absence of comparisons with topology-only baselines undermines claims of unique advantages.  

### Strengths:  
1. **Clarity and Presentation:** The paper is well-structured and clearly written, making the technical approach accessible.  
2. **High Missing Feature Robustness:** FP achieves commendable performance even when 99% of features are missing, a challenging scenario for many existing methods.  
3. **Computational Efficiency:** The reported speed improvements and scalability are promising, particularly for large-scale graphs.  

### Weaknesses and Major Concerns:  
1. **Limited Novelty:** The methodological derivation of FP closely mirrors Label Propagation (LP). Both minimize Dirichlet energy via iterative diffusion updates, differing primarily in the continuous feature space (FP) versus discrete labels (LP). This resemblance raises fundamental questions about innovation beyond existing LP frameworks.  
2. **Insufficient Baseline Comparisons:** Experiments omit critical comparisons with graph *topology-only* methods (e.g., LP, node2vec, DeepWalk). Without this, it is unclear whether FP’s gains stem from feature propagation or merely reflect inherent topological information.  
3. **Questionable Necessity:** At moderate missing rates (≤50%), simple neighbor averaging performs competitively. The paper does not convincingly establish why FP is needed outside of extreme (90-99%) missingness scenarios.  

### Technical Feedback:  
The derivation of FP (Section 3) parallels LP’s formalism—both employ iterative diffusion equations derived from energy minimization principles. While substituting labels with features is straightforward, this adaptation does not constitute a significant conceptual leap. The technical overlap with LP warrants a more rigorous justification of novelty.  

### Recommendations for Improvement:  
1. **Validate Against Topology Baselines:** Add experiments comparing FP with topology-only methods (LP, node2vec, DeepWalk) under *identical* extreme missing-feature settings (e.g., 99% missing). This is essential to isolate whether FP’s benefits are truly attributable to feature propagation.  
2. **Clarify Scope of Advantage:** Explicitly analyze scenarios where FP outperforms simpler methods (e.g., neighbor averaging) versus where gains are marginal. A discussion on the "sweet spot" for FP’s utility would strengthen the practical impact.  

### Decision:  
While FP demonstrates promising empirical results for extreme missing-feature cases and is well-executed technically, the lack of novelty relative to LP and insufficient experimental validation against topology-based baselines weaken its contribution. The work is **marginally below the acceptance threshold** in its current form. I encourage the authors to address the comparisons suggested above to better substantiate their claims of methodological innovation and practical utility.

title: Learning Audio-Visual Dereverberation

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper examines an audio-visual approach for dereverberation, where dereverberation of speech is conditioned on RGB and depth images (either field-of-view or panoramic). It proposes a dataset for this task based on real-world 3D scans of homes, using Librispeech data. Real data is also used The model is based on a U-Net conditioned on embeddings extracted by a "visual acoustics" network doing direct spectrogram prediction. The method is evaluated using PESQ, WER for speech recognition, and EER for speaker verification on synthetic and real data. The audio-visual method is found to perform marginally better than an audio-only version of the model. The audio-only version of the model outperforms several baselines from the literature on synthetic data, with mixed results on real data.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths:

S1) To my knowledge, this is the first audio-visual approach to dereverberation. I think it is interesting to see how much visual information can help.

S2) The proposed dataset seems quite useful. A lot of existing synthetic reverb for such audio tasks is generated using shoebox room simulators, so having more realistic environments seems quite useful. The accompanying data recorded in real environments using loudspeaker playback (such that the clean reference is available) is also quite useful, and I hope the authors plan to release a recipe for this data.

S3) The paper includes a lot of ablations, which I really appreciate. Though the focus of the paper is on audio-visual approaches to the task, I'm really glad the paper evaluates audio-only versions of the model, as well as other components such as the matching loss.

S4) The paper is clear and well-written.

### Weaknesses:

W1) I listened to the demos in the supplementary material, and I feel like I heard a fair amount of artifacts and distortion in some examples: for moderate reverb, the model predictions have a certain "buzziness" to them, and more extreme levels of reverb have a "twanginess" to them. Something that would make this paper stronger would be a subjective human listening evaluation, e.g. MUSHRA, which would measure these kinds of artifacts better than an objective measure like PESQ. Also, alternative loss functions may help reduce artifacts. E.g. L1 loss instead of L2 on spectrogram magnitude, or MSE on power-compressed spectrograms can provide more perceptually-relevant loss functions.

W2) The processing method of using 2.56s frames, then concatenating the non-overlapping parts together seems suboptimal to me. Why not use e.g. a Hann window to stitch the predicted patches with overlap-add, or invert back to the time-domain and do overlap-add and train through this?

W3) Where does the "random speech embedding sampled from the data batch." come from? How is it computed? Is this a speaker ID embedding?

W4) The improvements by adding visual information are marginal compared to the audio-only model. The audio-only model does perform better than the baselines, but degrades PESQ compared to other baselines on real data. To benchmark the audio-only baseline against the literature, it would be interesting to compare to competitive systems in e.g. the REVERB challenge. I'm also not very convinced that visual input really helps that much.

W5) Table 1 would be improved by adding trainable parameter counts. Is the proposed model only doing better because it has more trainable parameters? WPE itself has no trainable parameters, since it's just a model-based EM algorithm.

W6) The lack of noise in the training and eval data limits real-world applicability, but I understand the focus of the paper in only on dereverberation. I'm glad that the authors tested noise robustness with WHAM! data, but 20 dB is a pretty high SNR for noise robustness. It would be interesting to see how performance degrades with decreasing SNR.

### Minor comments

M1) I feel that this statement isn't quite right: "R is a function of...the relative positioning of the speaker and the listener." I would argue that an RIR can be a function of the absolute positioning of the speaker and the listener. E.g. what if a source is close to a wall, versus in the the middle of a room?

M2) For the real data, would it be possible to be more specific about the source-to-microphone distances used in the real recordings, beyond "near-field to mid-field to far-field."?

M3) Also concerning real data: would it be possible to more more specific for this statement? "For each location, we play around 10 utterances.". How many utterances? Gender balanced? Different speakers across rooms?

M4) Nit: U-Net instead of UNet?

M5) "While our model is agnostic to the audio source type,": depends on the training data.

M6) In Tables 1 and 2, I would say "anechoic speech (upper bound)" instead of "clean speech (upper bound)", because to me "clean" suggests there's additive noise.

M7) About the blind RT60/DRR/distance predictions: "After converging, the average errors for them are 3e-3s, 160 dB and 17.92m respectively." Are these absolute or squared errors?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall, I vote for marginal acceptance. This is an interesting novel task (audio-visual dereverberation), and the proposed synthetic and real data are quite interesting and useful for the community. But the paper's results show that visual input provides marginal performance gain over the audio-only baseline. The audio-only baseline outperforms some benchmarks in terms of PESQ, WER for speech recognition, and EER for speaker verification on synthetic data, and WER and EER for real data, but I have concerns about the subjective listening quality of the demos, compared to other dereverberation algorithms I have listened to. Thus, I am concerned that this isn't a significant improvement over audio-only prior work, at least in terms of subjective listening quality.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 4: The contributions are significant, and do not exist in prior works.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.
